text: So as all of you know, there are many ways to surgically correct a parasophageal hernia. In this video, I'm going to describe my preferred method, which is a robotic-assisted repair with an anterior fundoplication, and I'll try to describe my logic behind my decision as the video goes along. So the patient's an elderly woman with a classic type III parasophageal hernia. with severe symptoms. You can see here on the coronal cuts where the fundus and the GE junction is situated above the diaphragm. And here are the sagittal cuts. There you see the GE junction. She has regurgitation and reflux.

The surgical setup includes four robotic arms with a 30-degree down camera positioned to the left of the umbilicus, avoiding the falciform ligament. An 8mm interseal assistant port is placed on the lateral edge, and the patient is in a steep reverse Trendelenburg position. A Nathanson liver retractor is used in the subxiphoid space to retract the liver anteriorly. The camera provides a view of the port setup as the operation begins.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to dissect through the gastrohepatic omentum, carefully reducing the parasophageal hernia with the robotic fourth arm. The dissection targets the space between the right crus of the diaphragm and the hernia sac, avoiding excessive traction to prevent tearing the stomach's serosa. No significant branches of the left hepatic artery are visible, allowing straightforward cautery application.

The surgeon identifies the plane between the hernia sac and the diaphragm's crura, noting the separation of the right and left crura posteriorly. The left crus of the diaphragm is dissected using bipolar cautery, while the table-side assistant pulls the stomach down and to the left, and the surgeon's fourth arm pulls everything to the right.

The surgeon dissects the space between the gastroesophageal junction and the left crus of the diaphragm, maneuvering the fourth arm underneath the esophagus at the GE junction. A Penrose drain is placed around the esophagus at the angle of His, serving as a gentle retractor to provide traction and facilitate further dissection. The assistant pulls the stomach towards the patient's left, enhancing exposure and allowing for improved visualization of the posterior mediastinum.

The surgeon punctures into the right pleural space, visible in the surgical field. Using a vessel sealer extend, the surgeon divides the connective tissue attachments between the esophagus and mediastinal structures, maintaining a margin to avoid the posterior vagus nerve. The Penrose drain is used to retract the esophagus, while the assistant pulls down on the gastric fat pad. The surgeon aims to achieve 3-4 centimeters of esophageal length below the diaphragm, ensuring it is free from connective tissue attachments.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm and an assistant to expose the surgical field with a thirty-degree down camera. They divide connective tissue attachments near the esophagus, avoiding entry into the left pleural space. The surgeon carefully dissects omental fat adhered to the underside of the diaphragm near the left crus, preparing the area for reinforcement with bioabsorbable mesh. The confluence of the crura is visible as the surgeon cleans the area for mesh placement.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer extend to dissect and excise the gastric fat pad, staying close to the stomach wall to avoid compromising the anterior vagus nerve. The gastric fat pad is removed, revealing a clear view of the angle of His, which is positioned below the diaphragm without tension.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to mobilize the esophagus, gaining additional length while carefully avoiding the vagus nerve. The descending aorta is visible in the background, and the surgeon maintains a safe margin from the esophagus wall to protect the nerve. The robotic fourth arm is used to expose the surgical field, revealing connective tissue attachments and omental fat near the gastroesophageal junction. The angle of His is clearly defined, with the vagus nerve curling up to the left.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the Penrose drain to retract the esophagus while holding the omentum away with the fourth arm. Simple stitches using zero ethibond are placed around the exposed crura, preserving the peritoneal cell layer for stitch integrity. The Vessel Sealer Extend is used as a needle driver and suture cutter, with the table-side assistant tying knots using a TK knot pusher. Bioabsorbable mesh is prepared for reinforcement of the hernia repair.

The surgeon places posterior stitches to reinforce the hernia repair, noting their importance in preventing recurrence. An anterior stitch is added for additional support, creating a snug closure around the esophagus. The vessel sealer extend is used to manage omental fat and prepare the area for mesh placement. The bioabsorbable mesh, shaped in a U-fashion, is laid around the posterior crura and tacked to the diaphragm with stitches, ensuring it lies flush for optimal adhesion.

The surgeon places a posterior anchoring stitch on the diaphragm's crus, securing the mesh in position. The angle of His and gastroesophageal junction are visible, with three to four centimeters of esophageal length. The surgeon begins an anterior partial fundoplication, stitching between the stomach and esophagus at the GE junction, incorporating the diaphragm and mesh for a gastropexy.

The surgeon completes a partial anterior fundoplication, retrieving omental fat and hernia sac contents through a small bag. An esophagus scope is inserted to inspect the repair internally, ensuring the gastroesophageal junction is positioned below the diaphragm. The stomach is suctioned, concluding the procedure.
